A-001-001-001: In an RL circuit the time constant τ = L/R is the time for the current to rise to 63.2% of its final value — one time constant. The key word is current: an inductor opposes a sudden change in current, so it builds up gradually, which rules out the distractors that talk about voltage. 36.8% is the leftover fraction in a decay (the complement of 63.2%), not the rising value.
A-001-001-002: Reaching 63.2% of the final value always takes exactly one time constant; for an RC circuit τ = R × C. The figure 63.2% is 1 − 1/e, the defining one-time-constant level. The other phrases ('exponential rate', 'time factor', 'exponential period') are not the accepted term.
A-001-001-003: One time constant is the time for the current to reach 63.2% of its final value; for an RL circuit τ = L/R. 63.2% (which is 1 − 1/e) is the standard one-time-constant point. The alternative phrasings are not recognized terminology.
A-001-001-004: Discharging to 36.8% of the starting charge takes one time constant, because after one τ a decaying quantity falls to 1/e ≈ 36.8% of its initial value. Charging rises to 63.2% while discharging falls to 36.8% — the two are complements. The other phrases are not the recognized term.
A-001-001-005: Back EMF is a voltage that opposes the applied EMF (Lenz's law). When the current through an inductor changes, the inductor induces a voltage that fights that change; it is a voltage, not a current, which eliminates the current-based options. This opposition is exactly why current in an inductor builds up gradually rather than instantly.
A-001-001-006: After n time constants a charging capacitor reaches (1 − e⁻ⁿ) of the supply voltage. For n = 2: 1 − e⁻² = 1 − 0.135 = 0.865, i.e. 86.5%. 63.2% is the level after just one time constant and 36.8% is the fraction still remaining, so both are traps.
A-001-001-007: A discharging capacitor falls to e⁻ⁿ of its starting voltage after n time constants. For n = 2: e⁻² = 0.135, i.e. 13.5% remaining. 36.8% applies after only one time constant, and 86.5% is the charging (complement) value, so neither fits two time constants of discharge.
A-001-001-008: The time constant is τ = R × C. Convert first: 470 kΩ = 470 000 Ω and 100 µF = 100×10⁻⁶ F, so τ = 470 000 × 100×10⁻⁶ = 47 seconds. The wrong answers come from slipping the decimal point, so always convert kΩ and µF to ohms and farads before multiplying.
A-001-001-009: Time constant τ = R × C = 470 000 Ω × 470×10⁻⁶ F = 220.9 ≈ 221 seconds. Convert 470 kΩ → 470 000 Ω and 470 µF → 470×10⁻⁶ F before multiplying. The much larger answers come from leaving the values in kΩ or µF.
A-001-001-010: Time constant τ = R × C = 470 000 Ω × 220×10⁻⁶ F = 103.4 ≈ 103 seconds. Remember to convert 470 kΩ → 470 000 Ω and 220 µF → 220×10⁻⁶ F. Mishandling those powers of ten produces the distractor values.
A-001-002-001: Skin effect means that as frequency increases, RF current crowds into a thinner layer near the conductor's surface. It increases with frequency (not decreases, as one distractor claims) and is caused by induced eddy currents and self-inductance, not by heating. The thinner the effective conducting layer, the higher the conductor's RF resistance.
A-001-002-002: Skin effect is the phenomenon that forces RF current to flow mostly along the conductor's surface. The piezoelectric effect concerns mechanical stress in crystals, and resonance concerns reactive circuits, so neither applies. 'Layer effect' is not a real term.
A-001-002-003: Because of skin effect, almost all RF current flows along the surface of the conductor, not through its centre. Magnetic fields exist around a current-carrying wire, but the current itself travels in a thin surface layer at radio frequencies. This is why hollow tubing or silver plating works as well as solid wire for RF.
A-001-002-004: This surface crowding is skin effect, driven by self-inductance that increasingly impedes current toward the conductor's centre as frequency rises. The DC resistance is actually lower than the RF resistance (the opposite of one distractor), because at DC the whole cross-section carries current. Interior heating is a consequence in some cases, not the cause.
A-001-002-005: Skin effect makes a conductor's RF resistance higher than its DC resistance, because RF current uses only a thin surface layer instead of the full cross-section. With less effective copper carrying the current, resistance rises with frequency. The 'Hertzberg effect' and conductor non-linearity are not the cause, and insulation does not carry the current.
A-001-002-006: The farad (F) is the unit of capacitance — how much charge a capacitor stores per volt (C = Q/V). The coulomb measures charge itself, the volt measures potential, and the watt measures power, so none of those describe storage capacity. One farad equals one coulomb per volt.
A-001-002-007: A wire carrying a current is surrounded by a magnetic field (the magnetic part of the electromagnetic field) that circles the conductor. An electrostatic field arises from static charge or a voltage difference, not from current flow, so that option is wrong. The field strengthens with more current and weakens with distance.
A-001-002-008: The magnetic field circles the conductor, and its direction relative to electron flow is given by the left-hand rule: point the thumb along the electron flow and the curled fingers show the field direction. (Using conventional current, which flows opposite to electrons, you would use the right-hand rule instead.) The field is not in the same direction as the current and not in all directions, so those options are wrong.
A-001-002-009: Energy stored in an electric or magnetic field is potential energy — energy held by virtue of configuration and ready to do work. Kinetic energy is the energy of motion, which does not describe a static stored field. 'Ampere-joules' and 'joule-coulombs' are not real units of energy.
A-001-002-010: Between a capacitor's charged plates is an electrostatic (electric) field, created by the voltage difference across them. No steady current flows through an ideal dielectric, and stored charge does not create a magnetic field, so those options are wrong. This electric field is where the capacitor's energy is stored.
A-001-002-011: Energy stored in an inductor is W = ½LI², so besides current it depends on the inductance L, whose unit is the henry. The farad is for capacitance and the coulomb for charge, so they don't apply to an inductor. The watt is a unit of power, not the property that sets stored energy.
A-001-003-001: Resonant frequency is f = 1 / (2π√(LC)), identical for series and parallel RLC. With L = 50 µH = 50×10⁻⁶ H and C = 40 pF = 40×10⁻¹² F, LC = 2×10⁻¹⁵ and √(LC) ≈ 4.47×10⁻⁸, so f ≈ 1/(2π × 4.47×10⁻⁸) ≈ 3.56 MHz. R does not appear in the formula, and the kHz options are unit-scale traps.
A-001-003-002: Use f = 1 / (2π√(LC)). With L = 40×10⁻⁶ H and C = 200×10⁻¹² F, LC = 8×10⁻¹⁵ and √(LC) ≈ 8.94×10⁻⁸, so f ≈ 1.78 MHz. Keeping the 10⁻⁶ and 10⁻¹² factors straight is what lands the answer in MHz rather than the kHz traps.
A-001-003-003: Use f = 1 / (2π√(LC)). With L = 50×10⁻⁶ H and C = 10×10⁻¹² F, LC = 5×10⁻¹⁶ and √(LC) ≈ 2.24×10⁻⁸, giving f ≈ 7.12 MHz. Resistance is not in the formula, so it has no effect on resonance.
A-001-003-004: Use f = 1 / (2π√(LC)). With L = 25×10⁻⁶ H and C = 10×10⁻¹² F, LC = 2.5×10⁻¹⁶ and √(LC) ≈ 1.58×10⁻⁸, so f ≈ 10.1 MHz. The kHz answers are off by a factor of 1000 — a unit trap.
A-001-003-005: Use f = 1 / (2π√(LC)). With L = 3×10⁻⁶ H and C = 40×10⁻¹² F, LC = 1.2×10⁻¹⁶ and √(LC) ≈ 1.10×10⁻⁸, giving f ≈ 14.5 MHz. Only L and C set resonance, so the resistor value is irrelevant.
A-001-003-006: Use f = 1 / (2π√(LC)). With L = 4×10⁻⁶ H and C = 20×10⁻¹² F, LC = 8×10⁻¹⁷ and √(LC) ≈ 8.94×10⁻⁹, so f ≈ 17.8 MHz. Track the powers of ten carefully to avoid the kHz distractors.
A-001-003-007: Use f = 1 / (2π√(LC)). With L = 8×10⁻⁶ H and C = 7×10⁻¹² F, LC = 5.6×10⁻¹⁷ and √(LC) ≈ 7.48×10⁻⁹, giving f ≈ 21.3 MHz. The answer is in MHz once the µH and pF factors are handled correctly.
A-001-003-008: Use f = 1 / (2π√(LC)). With L = 3×10⁻⁶ H and C = 15×10⁻¹² F, LC = 4.5×10⁻¹⁷ and √(LC) ≈ 6.71×10⁻⁹, so f ≈ 23.7 MHz. Resistance does not enter the resonance formula.
A-001-003-009: Use f = 1 / (2π√(LC)). With L = 4×10⁻⁶ H and C = 8×10⁻¹² F, LC = 3.2×10⁻¹⁷ and √(LC) ≈ 5.66×10⁻⁹, giving f ≈ 28.1 MHz. Keep the 10⁻⁶ and 10⁻¹² straight to stay in MHz.
A-001-003-010: Use f = 1 / (2π√(LC)). With L = 1×10⁻⁶ H and C = 9×10⁻¹² F, LC = 9×10⁻¹⁸ and √(LC) = 3×10⁻⁹, so f ≈ 1/(2π × 3×10⁻⁹) ≈ 53.1 MHz. The small L and C push resonance high into VHF.
A-001-003-011: Rearrange f = 1/(2π√(LC)) to C = 1 / ((2πf)² × L). With f = 14.25×10⁶ Hz, 2πf ≈ 8.95×10⁷ and (2πf)² ≈ 8.0×10¹⁵; dividing by L = 2.84×10⁻⁶ H gives C ≈ 44×10⁻¹² F = 44 pF. The microfarad options are off by 10⁶, a unit trap.
A-001-004-001: For a parallel RLC circuit the resonant frequency is still f = 1 / (2π√(LC)); R does not affect it. With L = 1×10⁻⁶ H and C = 10×10⁻¹² F, LC = 1×10⁻¹⁷ and √(LC) ≈ 3.16×10⁻⁹, so f ≈ 50.3 MHz. The 4.7 kΩ is a red herring for the resonance calculation.
A-001-004-002: Resonance is f = 1 / (2π√(LC)) (R has no effect). With L = 2×10⁻⁶ H and C = 15×10⁻¹² F, LC = 3×10⁻¹⁷ and √(LC) ≈ 5.48×10⁻⁹, so f ≈ 29.1 MHz. The kHz answers are a factor-of-1000 trap.
A-001-004-003: Resonance is f = 1 / (2π√(LC)); the resistor doesn't change it. With L = 5×10⁻⁶ H and C = 9×10⁻¹² F, LC = 4.5×10⁻¹⁷ and √(LC) ≈ 6.71×10⁻⁹, giving f ≈ 23.7 MHz. Only L and C determine resonant frequency.
A-001-004-004: Resonance is f = 1 / (2π√(LC)). With L = 2×10⁻⁶ H and C = 30×10⁻¹² F, LC = 6×10⁻¹⁷ and √(LC) ≈ 7.75×10⁻⁹, so f ≈ 20.5 MHz. R is irrelevant to the resonant frequency.
A-001-004-005: Resonance is f = 1 / (2π√(LC)). With L = 15×10⁻⁶ H and C = 5×10⁻¹² F, LC = 7.5×10⁻¹⁷ and √(LC) ≈ 8.66×10⁻⁹, giving f ≈ 18.4 MHz. Watch the kHz distractors created by mishandling µH and pF.
A-001-004-006: Resonance is f = 1 / (2π√(LC)). With L = 3×10⁻⁶ H and C = 40×10⁻¹² F, LC = 1.2×10⁻¹⁶ and √(LC) ≈ 1.10×10⁻⁸, so f ≈ 14.5 MHz. The resistor does not enter the formula.
A-001-004-007: Resonance is f = 1 / (2π√(LC)). With L = 40×10⁻⁶ H and C = 6×10⁻¹² F, LC = 2.4×10⁻¹⁶ and √(LC) ≈ 1.55×10⁻⁸, so f ≈ 10.3 MHz. Only L and C set the resonant frequency.
A-001-004-008: Resonance is f = 1 / (2π√(LC)). With L = 10×10⁻⁶ H and C = 50×10⁻¹² F, LC = 5×10⁻¹⁶ and √(LC) ≈ 2.24×10⁻⁸, giving f ≈ 7.12 MHz. The kHz options are off by a factor of 1000.
A-001-004-009: Resonance is f = 1 / (2π√(LC)). With L = 200×10⁻⁶ H and C = 10×10⁻¹² F, LC = 2×10⁻¹⁵ and √(LC) ≈ 4.47×10⁻⁸, so f ≈ 3.56 MHz. The large inductance pulls the resonant frequency down to the 80 m region.
A-001-004-010: Resonance is f = 1 / (2π√(LC)). With L = 90×10⁻⁶ H and C = 100×10⁻¹² F, LC = 9×10⁻¹⁵ and √(LC) ≈ 9.49×10⁻⁸, giving f ≈ 1.68 MHz. Keep the µH and pF powers of ten straight to avoid the kHz traps.
A-001-004-011: Rearrange f = 1/(2π√(LC)) to L = 1 / ((2πf)² × C). With f = 14.25×10⁶ Hz, (2πf)² ≈ 8.0×10¹⁵; dividing by C = 44×10⁻¹² F gives L ≈ 2.8×10⁻⁶ H = 2.8 µH. The millihenry options are off by a factor of 1000 or more — unit traps.
A-001-005-001: For a parallel resonant circuit, Q = R / (2πfL) = R / X_L. Here X_L = 2π × 14.128×10⁶ × 2.7×10⁻⁶ ≈ 240 Ω, so Q = 18 000 / 240 ≈ 75.1. This is the parallel form, where Q rises with R; the series form Q = X_L/R would give a wrong, tiny value.
A-001-005-002: Parallel Q = R / (2πfL). X_L = 2π × 14.128×10⁶ × 4.7×10⁻⁶ ≈ 417 Ω, so Q = 18 000 / 417 ≈ 43.1. A larger inductance means a larger X_L and thus a lower Q for the same R.
A-001-005-003: Parallel Q = R / (2πfL). X_L = 2π × 4.468×10⁶ × 47×10⁻⁶ ≈ 1320 Ω, so Q = 180 / 1320 ≈ 0.136. A small R with a large X_L gives a very low Q; the answers above 1 come from inverting the ratio.
A-001-005-004: Parallel Q = R / (2πfL). X_L = 2π × 14.225×10⁶ × 3.5×10⁻⁶ ≈ 313 Ω, so Q = 10 000 / 313 ≈ 31.9. Use the parallel form (R divided by X_L), not the series form.
A-001-005-005: Parallel Q = R / (2πfL). X_L = 2π × 7.125×10⁶ × 8.2×10⁻⁶ ≈ 367 Ω, so Q = 1000 / 367 ≈ 2.73. Here R is only a few times X_L, so the Q is modest.
A-001-005-006: Parallel Q = R / (2πfL). X_L = 2π × 7.125×10⁶ × 10.1×10⁻⁶ ≈ 452 Ω, so Q = 100 / 452 ≈ 0.221. Because R is smaller than X_L, the Q is below 1.
A-001-005-007: Parallel Q = R / (2πfL). X_L = 2π × 7.125×10⁶ × 12.6×10⁻⁶ ≈ 564 Ω, so Q = 22 000 / 564 ≈ 39. The large R relative to X_L gives a high Q.
A-001-005-008: Parallel Q = R / (2πfL). X_L = 2π × 3.625×10⁶ × 3×10⁻⁶ ≈ 68.3 Ω, so Q = 2200 / 68.3 ≈ 32.2. A small inductive reactance with a large R yields a high Q.
A-001-005-009: Parallel Q = R / (2πfL). X_L = 2π × 3.625×10⁶ × 42×10⁻⁶ ≈ 957 Ω, so Q = 220 / 957 ≈ 0.23. A small R against a large X_L gives a Q well below 1.
A-001-005-010: Parallel Q = R / (2πfL). X_L = 2π × 3.625×10⁶ × 43×10⁻⁶ ≈ 979 Ω, so Q = 1800 / 979 ≈ 1.84. Remember to divide R by X_L for a parallel circuit.
A-001-005-011: Bandwidth = f / Q, so lowering Q widens the bandwidth. Adding a resistor (a deliberate loss/load) to a parallel resonant circuit lowers its Q and therefore broadens its response. A resistor cannot raise Q, and it does not change the resonant frequency (set by L and C), which eliminates the other options.
